Revised the section for prose clarity: broke run-on sentences, normalized tense to the simple past/present for reported results, removed conversational hedges, and tightened phrasing. No claims, numbers, or citations were altered.

---

In this section we describe the multi-agent retrieval framework used to evaluate compositional question answering. We adopt a planner–retriever–reader decomposition, following conventions established in recent work on agentic search. The planner issues sub-queries; the retriever returns candidate passages from a BM25 index built over the MS MARCO passage corpus; and the reader synthesizes the final answer from the retrieved evidence. We instantiate the framework as a three-agent topology and evaluate it on a held-out slice of the BEIR benchmark suite curated over the preceding quarter.

For retrieval, we use a hybrid dense–sparse fusion of BM25 and a ColBERT-v2 late-interaction model. Fusion weights are learned on a validation split sampled from the training set, and the resulting fused scorer is applied at test time to obtain the top-k candidate passages consumed by the reader. The planner decomposes each input query into between two and five sub-queries, with the count determined by a learned structural-complexity classifier. We observe that the planner occasionally produces redundant sub-queries, which the retriever deduplicates before forwarding the consolidated passage set.

We measure end-to-end answer accuracy with exact match and token-level F1, and additionally report nDCG@10 for the retrieval stage. The multi-agent system improves over the single-agent baseline by 4.3 F1 points on the multi-hop subset, compared with a smaller gain of approximately 1.1 points on single-hop questions. This gap suggests that agentic decomposition contributes most where compositional reasoning is required.

The planner–retriever interface introduces non-trivial latency, which we were unable to fully amortize through caching at submission time; the throughput numbers in Table 3 reflect this overhead. We plan to address it in future iterations of the pipeline.
